5: A fulfillment: "Sacrifices and offerings you have not desired, but a body you have prepared for me... I have come to do your will, O God." The Son speaks these words at his entry into the world (incarnation), applying Psalms 40:6-8 to himself. The body prepared for the Son is the instrument of the will-doing that replaces and fulfills what the animal sacrifices only anticipated. The incarnation is the fulfillment of the Psalm's declaration: the one who comes to do God's will is the Son, the will he does is his obedient self-offering, and the body he does it in is the one God prepared for him at Bethlehem.
10: A direct revelation: we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all. The sanctification accomplished by Christ's body-offering is a completed act (aorist-perfect: we have been sanctified) with permanent effect. The "once for all" (ephapax) is the theological key: it cannot be supplemented, repeated, or improved. Those who are in Christ are fully and permanently holy before God because of what Christ did — not partially holy pending further sacrifice or repeated ritual.
12: A direct revelation: Christ, after offering one sacrifice for sins forever, sat down at the right hand of God. The sitting down is the posture of completed work — no Levitical priest ever sat in the sanctuary (the work was never done). The right-hand session is the evidence of divine acceptance. The single sacrifice for sins forever — not "for now" or "for past sins" but for sins as a category, permanently and completely — is what the session declares complete. The community draws near on the basis of this completed sacrifice.
19: A direct revelation: we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh. The opened way is the Incarnation-death-resurrection-ascension sequence understood as Christ's path through the curtain into the Father's presence, and simultaneously as the path he has opened for all who follow him through his death and resurrection into the same divine presence. The "new and living way" contrasts with the Levitical way: new (in the new covenant sense — qualitatively different), living (because Christ is alive and the way is sustained by his living intercession).
25: A direct revelation: not neglecting to meet together, and all the more as you see the Day drawing near. The Day of the Lord — Christ's return — is the eschatological horizon that orients the community's present assembly. The assembly anticipates the great heavenly assembly (Hebrews 12:22-24) that the return will complete. The mutual encouragement of the assembly is the community's participation in what the Spirit and the Son are already doing from the right hand of God — drawing the community together in love toward the Day.
37: A fulfillment: "He who is coming will come and will not delay; but my righteous one shall live by faith." The "coming one" of Habakkuk 2:3 — the vision awaiting its appointed time — is fulfilled in the coming of Christ at his first advent and anticipated in his second. Hebrews applies the text to encourage a community waiting for the parousia: the same Habakkuk-logic applies — the vision (now: Christ's return) awaits its time, will certainly come, and the righteous are those who live by faith through the waiting rather than shrinking back.
1: A shadow: the law contains only a shadow (skian) of the good things that are coming, not the true form of those realities. Hebrews here names its own hermeneutical principle: the Levitical system is the shadow that the body of Christ casts backward into the old covenant. The shadow is real and instructive — it is the shape of what is coming — but it is not the thing itself. Christ is the substance that the annual sacrifices could only outline. The repetition of sacrifice was itself the evidence of the shadow-nature: a shadow must keep being redrawn because it is not the reality.
2: A revelation of God: if the sacrifices had achieved genuine cleansing, they would have ceased — the worshippers would have had no more consciousness of sin. The logic discloses what full atonement must accomplish: not the covering of sin pending further offerings, but the complete removal of guilt such that the conscience is permanently clear. This is what Christ achieves (Hebrews 10:22): the heart sprinkled clean and the conscience no longer accusing. The repetition of sacrifice was a perpetual announcement that full atonement had not yet come.
3: A shadow: the annual reminder (anamnēsis) of sins at Yom Kippur is the negative shadow — it announces what is needed rather than providing it. The Day of Atonement was not a celebration of accomplished forgiveness but a yearly acknowledgment of ongoing guilt. Christ is the antitype in which sins are not reminded but removed and remembered no more (Hebrews 10:17): his sacrifice is the anti-memory, the permanent removal that makes the annual reminder obsolete.
4: A revelation of God: the impossibility of animal blood removing sins discloses a necessary truth about what effective atonement requires. If bull and goat blood cannot remove sin, then the one whose blood can must be categorically different — not another creature offering, but the Son offering himself. The impossibility of the shadow is the inverse image of the necessity of the Incarnation: only the blood of one who is both truly divine and truly human can accomplish what the Levitical system announced but could not achieve.
6: A fulfillment: the continuation of the Psalm 40 citation — God's declared non-pleasure in burnt offerings even as they operated within his covenant. The prophetic tradition consistently signals (Isaiah 1:11; Jeremiah 6:20; Amos 5:21) that the sacrifices were never the ultimate aim but pointed beyond themselves. What God desires is the obedient will-doing of the Son. The citation reveals that the old covenant's own prophetic voice anticipated the replacement of the sacrificial apparatus with the one whom the apparatus foreshadowed.
7: A fulfillment: "Behold, I have come to do your will, O God, as it is written of me in the scroll of the book." The Son's entry into the world as the fulfillment of Psalms 40:8 — the words placed on his lips at incarnation. This is the theological key to Hebrews' doctrine of atonement: not an unwilling victim but the Son whose entire being is constituted by doing the Father's will. The atonement works because the Son willed it, submitted to it, and accomplished it as the eternally obedient Son in the form of a servant.
8: A fulfillment: the Psalm citation is summarized and its conclusion drawn — what God neither desired nor took pleasure in (despite the Mosaic ordinance commanding them) was the first covenant order of sacrifices. The double negation emphasizes that the old order was always provisional. The summary prepares for the interpretive conclusion: the author is not reporting a Mosaic failure but revealing that even within the old covenant God was pointing past it toward the one whose obedient will-doing was always the goal.
9: A direct revelation: Christ removes the first covenant order in order to establish the second. The abolition of the Levitical system is not historical accident or Roman-era disruption but a theological consequence of the Son's coming: the shadow is removed when the substance arrives. The "second" — Christ's will-doing, his body-offering, his eternal high priesthood — is not an improvisation but what the first always pointed toward. The removal of the first is the revelation that the first was designed to be removed when its purpose was accomplished.
11: A shadow: every Levitical priest stands day after day in service — the standing posture reveals what the sitting of Christ (Hebrews 10:12) means. No priest ever sat in the sanctuary because the work was never done; the standing was the posture of ongoing, incomplete service. The repeated same sacrifices that could never take away sins are the shadow by negative contrast: they announce by their inadequacy and perpetuity that something qualitatively different was required. The standing priest points to the sitting Christ.
13: A fulfillment: "until his enemies are made a footstool for his feet" (Psalms 110:1). The session involves active waiting for the eschatological subjugation of all enemies. The Christ who has completed atonement and is enthroned is now in the "until" phase of his reign — the kingdom is inaugurated but not yet consummated, the enemies are defeated in principle but not yet visibly subjected. The parousia is the moment of the enemies' footstool-making, the completion of what the resurrection-exaltation began.
14: A direct revelation: by a single offering he has brought to completion for all time those who are being made holy. The paradox of complete-in-Christ but still-being-made-holy-in-experience is the key to understanding sanctification: the atonement is not partial or provisional. Those in Christ have been brought to completion (teteleiōken, perfect tense: it stands completed) by the one offering. The present progressive "being sanctified" describes the application process, not the achievement — the achievement is already perfect and permanent.
15: A direct revelation: the Holy Spirit also bears witness to us. The Trinitarian structure of the community's assurance: the Son achieved the atonement; the Father made the new covenant promise; the Spirit testifies to both. The community's confidence rests not on their subjective experience but on the Spirit-attested word — the Spirit who spoke through Jeremiah now speaks through the new covenant fulfillment, bearing witness to the forgiveness Christ has secured.
16: A fulfillment: "I will put my laws on their hearts and write them on their minds" (Jeremiah 31:33). The new covenant promise is fulfilled in Christ: the law internalized by the Spirit is the eschatological gift that the old covenant announced but could not achieve. What stone tablets and animal sacrifices could not effect — inner transformation, willing obedience from renewed hearts — is given through the Spirit purchased by Christ's blood. The new covenant is not merely better legislation but a new creation of the person from within.
17: A fulfillment: "I will remember their sins and their lawless deeds no more" (Jeremiah 31:34). The divine not-remembering of sins is the final element of the new covenant's fulfillment. This is not amnesia but the consequence of genuine atonement: the debt has been paid, the record cleared, the guilt removed. Christ's sacrifice is the ground of this divine forgetting — not that God ignores sin, but that the sin has been fully dealt with by the one who bore it. The community stands before God with a permanently clear record.
18: A direct revelation: where there is forgiveness of sins, there is no longer any offering for sin. The logical conclusion of the atonement is the permanent obsolescence of the sacrificial system. If sins are genuinely forgiven — not merely deferred or covered — no further sacrifice is needed or can be offered. This reveals the completeness of Christ's work: the entire Levitical apparatus is not paused but ended, not by historical contingency but by theological consequence. The cross is the reason the temple's destruction matters.
20: A direct revelation: Christ opened for us a new and living way through the curtain, that is, through his flesh. The identification of the torn curtain with Christ's body is the key interpretive move: his death (the tearing of his flesh) is simultaneously the opening of access to the Father's presence. The curtain separated humanity from the divine holiness; Christ's body-sacrifice is the curtain-tearing that renders that separation obsolete. The way is "living" because Christ is alive and his ongoing intercession sustains the open access for those who come through him.
21: A direct revelation: we have a great high priest over the house of God. The enthroned Son functions as high priest not from the earthly shadow-sanctuary but from the heavenly reality, interceding continually for the community that is itself the house of God. The "great" priest does not merely exceed the Levitical high priests in degree but surpasses them in kind: they served in the earthly copy; he serves in the heavenly original; they offered animals; he offered himself; they entered once a year; he entered permanently.
22: A direct revelation: let us draw near with sincere hearts in full assurance of faith, with hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience and bodies washed with pure water. The invitation to draw near — which the Levitical curtain had barred for all but the high priest — is now extended to the whole covenant community. The conditions described (inner cleansing, outer identification) are the new covenant realities Christ secured: the conscience purified by his blood and the body marked by baptism into his death. The drawing-near is the community's participation in what Christ has achieved.
23: A direct revelation: let us hold firmly to the confession of our hope without wavering, for he who promised is faithful. The ground of the community's holding fast is not their own grip on the hope but the faithfulness of the one who promised — the same divine faithfulness that kept every covenant promise across the centuries and that is finally and definitively expressed in Christ's resurrection. The confession of hope is hope in Christ's return and the full realization of everything his atonement secured.
24: A revelation of God: let us consider how to stir one another on to love and good works. The horizontal ethic of mutual encouragement flows from the vertical reality of the atonement: because Christ has made the community one body before God, members share responsibility for one another's perseverance. Love and good works are not the ground of acceptance but the evidence of it — the life that the Spirit produces in those who have genuinely drawn near to God through Christ.
26: A revelation of God: if we sin deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins. The warning presupposes the fullness of what Christ has achieved: precisely because the sacrifice is once-for-all and complete, there is no second sacrifice for those who deliberately abandon it. The severity is proportional to the greatness of the provision: the richer the grace, the more catastrophic the deliberate rejection. This is not addressed to involuntary sin but to the willful apostasy of those who turn their backs on Christ knowing what they are rejecting.
27: A revelation of God: a terrifying expectation of judgment and a raging fire that will consume the adversaries of God. The consuming fire (Isaiah 26:11) is the character of divine holiness encountering undealt-with sin. What Christ absorbed in his death as the judgment-bearing sacrifice is what awaits those who reject him: the fire that should have consumed them falls instead on the one who had no sin, and those who reject him remain in the path of it. The judgment is real because the holiness is real.
28: A shadow: anyone who rejected the law of Moses died without mercy on the evidence of two or three witnesses. The Mosaic capital penalty for covenant-breaking is the shadow of the final judgment's logic: if the shadow of the first covenant brought death without mercy, how much more the reality of the new? The argument is a fortiori — from the lesser (Mosaic penalty) to the greater (eschatological judgment) — and it is the same argument used throughout Hebrews to establish Christ's supremacy.
29: A direct revelation: how much worse punishment will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God, treated his covenant blood as common, and outraged the Spirit of grace? The Trinitarian structure of the offense reveals the Trinitarian structure of the gospel: the Son is the mediator, his blood is the new covenant blood, the Spirit is the Spirit of grace through whom the gospel is received. To trample, treat-as-common, and outrage these three is to reject the entire economy of salvation. The punishment is incomparably greater because the provision was incomparably greater.
30: A revelation of God: "Vengeance is mine; I will repay" and "The Lord will judge his people" (Deuteronomy 32:35-36). The divine justice that judged Israel in the wilderness is the same justice that operates in the new covenant community. God who was faithful to judge his own people under Moses is faithful to judge those who betray the new covenant. This is not arbitrary wrath but the judicial faithfulness of the covenant God to his own standards — the same faithfulness that grounds both the promises and the warnings.
31: A revelation of God: it is a terrifying thing to fall into the hands of the living God. The living God — not an idol or an abstraction but the personally active, covenant-making, resurrection-working God — is the one before whom apostates will stand. The terror is not of an anonymous force but of the Father who provided the Son, whose provision has been knowingly rejected. The one who gives life has the power to withhold it; the one whose hands sustain all creation can also judge all creation.
32: A direct revelation: recall the earlier days when, after you were enlightened, you endured a hard struggle. The "enlightenment" (phōtisthentes) refers to conversion and baptism — entry into the new covenant community — and the persecution that followed was the expected response of the world to those who belong to Christ. The community's past endurance is evidence of genuine faith and a pattern for present faithfulness: those who endured then can endure now, because the same Christ who sustained them then sustains them now.
33: A theme: the theme of the reproach of Christ that runs through Hebrews (Hebrews 11:26; Hebrews 13:13) surfaces in the community's own experience — their public shaming and affliction are their participation in the same rejection that Christ himself received. To bear reproach alongside the people of God is to be identified with the one who bore reproach for them. The community's suffering is not merely unfortunate circumstance but the shape of their solidarity with the crucified Messiah.
34: A direct revelation: you joyfully accepted the plundering of your property, knowing that you yourselves had a better possession and an abiding one. The community's joy in earthly loss reveals the eschatological orientation that faith in Christ produces: the better and enduring possession is the heavenly inheritance — the city with foundations (Hebrews 11:10), the unshakeable kingdom (Hebrews 12:28) — that Christ has secured and that no earthly confiscation can touch. To lose what is temporary while holding what is permanent is the logic of resurrection faith.
35: A direct revelation: do not throw away your confidence, which carries a great reward. The confidence (parrēsia) is the bold access to the Father that Christ's atonement creates — the posture of approaching God as one who has been welcomed, not tentatively or anxiously but with the assurance of those whose guilt has been removed. The reward (misthapodosia) is the inheritance, the rest, the city that Abraham saw in faith — what the enduring community will receive when the Day comes.
36: A revelation of God: you need endurance, so that when you have done the will of God you may receive what was promised. The will of God here is not the moral law alone but the specifically covenantal will-doing that Christ modeled: faithful endurance through suffering toward the resurrection-reward. The community follows Christ in the same pattern — faith-obedience through trials that led him to the session at the right hand. The promise is certain; what is required is endurance in the path that Christ already walked.
38: A fulfillment: "My righteous one shall live by faith, and if he shrinks back, my soul takes no pleasure in him" (Habakkuk 2:4). The text is applied both to Christ (the supreme righteous one who lived by faith, Hebrews 12:2) and to the community that follows him. Christ endured to the end; those who belong to him are called to the same enduring faith. The shrinking back (hypostellē) is the abandonment of the bold access and the confession of hope — the opposite of what Christ modeled in his faithful endurance to the cross.
39: A direct revelation: we do not belong to those who shrink back and are destroyed, but to those who have faith and so preserve their souls. The positive assurance that closes the warning section: the author identifies himself and the community with the faithful, not the apostates. The preservation of the soul is not the fruit of heroic effort but of the faith that trusts the completed work of Christ and endures in hope toward the Day. The community's identity is established by this trust, not undermined by the warnings that address its opposite.
